nativeRange.habitat: Coastal coral and rocky reefs, often in silty or turbid lagoons; midwater schools typically at 1–30 m, recorded to 60 m

decorPreferences: live rock perimeter with caves and crevices

diet.notes: In the wild this species feeds primarily on zooplankton in midwater. In captivity, has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp, enriched frozen brine shrimp, krill, clam meat, and high-quality prepared meaty foods. Multiple sources describe this fish as a constant grazer; small portions fed four to six times per day are generally advised to match the species' high metabolism.

goodWith: reef fish of similar size and temperament

summary: A fast-swimming Indo-Pacific reef planktivore that schools in coastal waters; generally considered peaceful but reaches around 60 cm and is best kept in very large systems with ample horizontal swimming room.
careNotes:
Caesio cuning is a midwater schooling fusilier from coastal Indo-Pacific reefs. Adult coloration is silvery blue with a pink to reddish flush on the belly, a yellow caudal fin, and a small black spot at the pectoral fin base; juveniles offered for sale are typically much smaller than the documented maximum adult size.

Source spread on adult size is wide: LiveAquaria cites about 23 cm (9 in), while FishBase, Reeflex, and Quality Marine report a maximum total length of 50–60 cm. The 23–60 cm range here reflects that fish sold at retail size are usually well under their genetic maximum, but planning around adult size is generally advised.

Minimum tank size disagreement across sources is substantial: LiveAquaria recommends 300 US gal (~1136 L); Quality Marine recommends 250 US gal (~946 L); Reeflex recommends roughly 3000 L for a school. Because this species is a fast pelagic schooler that can approach 60 cm and is repeatedly described as needing large open swim areas, the figure has been biased toward the larger end and rounded to a nice value of 2000 L. Keepers committing to a long-term home for a school should still expect that requirements may grow further as fish mature.

Temperature recommendations vary: LiveAquaria 72–78°F (22–26°C); Quality Marine 72–78°F; Reeflex 23–28°C; FishBase wild range 26.1–29.1°C. Averaging across sources gives roughly 22–28°C.

Care-level ratings disagree across sources from 'easy' (LiveAquaria) to 'moderate' (Quality Marine) to 'only for advanced aquarists' (Reeflex). The middle value 'intermediate' has been taken here, but the very large tank footprint and constant-feeding requirement push the practical demands of this species toward advanced; tank capacity and feeding logistics are the limiting factors more than water-quality sensitivity.

This species has been reported as schooling in nature; small groups (at least three individuals) are generally advised in captivity. Multiple sources describe it as peaceful but unsuitable to house with slow or shy tankmates, which it can out-compete at feeding time. Reef compatibility is generally listed as 'with caution' — it is not known to nip corals, but its size and swimming speed can disturb sessile invertebrates and stress smaller tankmates.

This species has been documented jumping from open aquaria as a fast midwater swimmer; a tightly fitted lid is generally advised.

Lifespan figures are not well documented in hobby sources; FishBase reports a population-doubling time of 1.4–4.4 years, which is a fisheries-resilience metric, not a captive longevity figure. Lifespan is left null pending better data.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: Cross-source disagreements on tank size, temperature, and care level resolved by consulting FishBase and LiveAquaria afresh. (1) Tank size: 2000 L confirmed as defensible upper-biased nice value — FishBase confirms 60 cm TL max and schooling midwater behavior; no source contradicts the upper bias approach. (2) Temperature: 22–28°C retained as the broadest hobbyist-safe envelope spanning all sources (LiveAquaria 22–26°C, Reeflex 23–28°C, FishBase wild 26–29°C). (3) Care level: 'intermediate' retained — LiveAquaria says easy, Quality Marine moderate, Reeflex advanced; intermediate reflects the practical middle and the footnote explaining why practical demands skew advanced. (4) fish.constantGrazer set to true — FishBase planktivore data, LiveAquaria 'active grazer' language, and Quality Marine's ~6 feedings/day recommendation all confirm constant-grazing metabolism. (5) fish.feedingDifficulty set to 'easy' — LiveAquaria rates it easy and notes it accepts frozen zooplankton foods. (6) depthRangeM.max corrected to 60 m to match FishBase (habitat text already said 'recorded to 60 m'). (7) fish.sexChange: 'none' confirmed — FishBase lists oviparous gonochoristic reproduction with no hermaphroditism data; Wikipedia corroborates.
breedingNotes: Reported as oviparous in the wild, releasing numerous small pelagic eggs in midwater. Captive breeding has been mentioned as possible but is not common, and rearing larvae is reported to be impractical at hobby scale; captive supply is essentially all wild-caught.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for tank size (300 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), temperament, care level, and reef compatibility.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, family (Caesionidae), Indo-West Pacific native range (Sri Lanka to Vanuatu; southern Japan to northern Australia), maximum total length 60 cm, depth range 1–60 m, planktivorous diet, oviparous reproduction, wild temperature 26.1–29.1°C.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (50–60 cm), Indo-Pacific range, and aquarium recommendation of approximately 3000 L for a school; rates care difficulty as advanced. Used to bias minimum tank volume upward.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (~24 in / 60 cm), minimum tank (250 gal / 946 L), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), peaceful temperament, schooling behavior, and constant-grazing feeding pattern (~6 feedings per day).

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous broadcast spawner in the wild; captive breeding has been reported as possible but is not at scale, and larval rearing is not practical at hobby level.
